section.title: By Grace Through Faith
section.sectionRationale: Here the great blessings of chapter one come down to earth in the story of every believer. Paul describes the human condition in the starkest terms, dead in sin, enslaved to the world, the devil, and the flesh, and children of wrath, and then interrupts it with two of the most glorious words in Scripture: but God. Salvation is shown to be entirely God's gift of grace, received through faith, producing a people created for good works. This is the gospel in miniature.
section.thesis: We were spiritually dead, enslaved, and under wrath, but God, rich in mercy and great in love, made us alive with Christ, raised us, and seated us with Him, saving us by grace through faith as His own gift, so that no one can boast, and creating us anew in Christ for the good works He prepared beforehand.
section.frameNote: Three movements: our former state, dead in trespasses and by nature children of wrath; the great reversal, but God, rich in mercy, made us alive with Christ; and the summary, saved by grace through faith, God's gift, His workmanship created for good works.

units.label: Dead in sin
units.note: the world, the devil, and the flesh

verses.text: And you were {{nekros|dead}} in your trespasses and sins,
in which you used to walk when you conformed to the ways of this world and of the ruler of the power of the air, the spirit who is now at work in the sons of disobedience.
All of us also lived among them at one time, fulfilling the cravings of our flesh and indulging its desires and thoughts. Like the rest, we were by nature children of wrath.

units.label: But God, rich in mercy, made us alive

verses.text: But because of His great love for us, God, who is rich in mercy,
made us alive with Christ even when we were dead in our trespasses. It is by {{charis|grace}} you have been saved!
And God raised us up with Christ and seated us with Him in the heavenly realms in Christ Jesus,
in order that in the coming ages He might display the surpassing riches of His grace, demonstrated by His kindness to us in Christ Jesus.

units.label: Saved by grace through faith, created for good works

verses.text: For it is by grace you have been {{sozo|saved}} through faith, and this not from yourselves; it is the {{doron|gift}} of God,
not by works, so that no one can boast.
For we are God's {{poiema|workmanship}}, {{ktizo|created}} in Christ Jesus to do good works, which God prepared in advance as our way of life.

nekros.gloss: Dead, a corpse, without life. Paul does not say we were merely sick or weak in sin but dead in trespasses and sins, spiritually lifeless and unable to raise ourselves. This diagnosis is the dark backdrop against which grace shines: only the God who raises the dead can save people in such a state.

charis.gloss: Grace, free and unearned favor, a gift given with no thought of repayment. It is the great keyword of the passage, breaking in with by grace you have been saved. Grace is God's kindness shown to those who deserve wrath, and Paul insists it is the whole ground of salvation, ruling out any human boast.

sozo.gloss: To save, rescue, deliver, and make whole. Notably Paul uses the perfect tense, you have been saved, marking salvation as an accomplished reality with lasting effect, not a hope still hanging in the balance. The rescue is from death, wrath, and bondage into life with Christ.

doron.gloss: A gift, something freely given. Salvation is the gift of God, not a wage earned or a prize won. The word underlines that the whole of this rescue, from first to last, originates in God's generosity and is received with empty hands, leaving no room for pride.

poiema.gloss: That which is made, a work or masterpiece, from which the English word poem derives. Believers are God's own handiwork, crafted by Him. The word frames the Christian life as God's creative project: we do not make ourselves, we are made new by the Maker for a purpose He has set.

ktizo.gloss: To create, the verb used for God's original act of creation. To be created in Christ Jesus is to be made new, a fresh act of divine creation bringing life where there was death. The same power that made the world remakes the believer for the good works God has prepared in advance.

basic.context: Paul now tells the story of every Christian. We began dead in our sins, walking in step with the world, following the devil, driven by our own cravings, and standing under God's wrath by nature. Then come the two greatest words: but God. Because of His great love and rich mercy, God made us alive with Christ, raised us, and seated us with Him. Salvation is by grace through faith, entirely God's gift, so that no one can boast, and we are His new creation, made for good works He planned long ago.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage moves in three beats: our hopeless condition, God's merciful intervention, and the nature of the salvation He gives. The turning point is the phrase but God in verse 4. Read it as a rescue story in which the rescued contribute nothing but the deadness that needed reviving. The good works of verse 10 are the goal and result of salvation, never its cause.
basic.originalLanguages: We were nekros, dead, in trespasses and sins, lifeless and helpless. But God, rich in mercy, saved us by charis, grace, unearned favor. Paul says you have been sozo, saved, an accomplished rescue that stands. Salvation is the doron, the gift, of God, not of works. And we are His poiema, His workmanship or masterpiece, ktizo, created anew, in Christ Jesus for good works.
basic.typology: Being made alive, raised, and seated with Christ ties the believer's story to Christ's own death, resurrection, and enthronement, so that His history becomes ours. Creation in Christ echoes the first creation in Genesis, when God made humanity and called it good; now, in Christ, God performs a new creation, bringing life out of the death sin had caused.
basic.christ: Everything happens with Christ and in Christ: we are made alive with Him, raised with Him, and seated with Him in the heavenly realms. His resurrection and enthronement, described in chapter one, are shared with all who are joined to Him. Christ is the sphere of the new creation, the one in whom the dead are made alive and remade for good works.
basic.god: God is the hero of this passage: rich in mercy, great in love, and lavish in grace. He acts while we are still dead, moved by nothing outside Himself but His own love. His purpose reaches into the coming ages, that He might display the surpassing riches of His grace forever. He is a God who saves the helpless and gets all the glory.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 5 says that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us, and that God shows His love to the ungodly. Colossians 2 speaks of being made alive together with Christ, forgiven all our trespasses. Titus 3 declares that God saved us not by works but according to His mercy.
basic.oneStory: This is the gospel in miniature, the movement of the whole Bible from death to life. The fall left humanity dead in sin and under wrath, but God's rescuing love, promised from Genesis onward, breaks in with but God. The new creation begun here points to the day when all things are made new, a people raised with Christ and walking in the good works their Maker prepared.

context.literary: Verses 1 to 10 form one movement built on a sharp contrast. Verses 1 to 3 describe the plight, a long buildup with no main verb, leaving the reader suspended in death, until the great turn at but God in verse 4. Verses 4 to 7 describe God's saving action in a series of with Christ verbs, and verses 8 to 10 summarize the whole as grace, through faith, unto good works. The suspended grammar itself dramatizes helplessness relieved only by God.
context.historical: Paul writes to a mixed church in a city dominated by pagan religion, magic, and the imperial cult. The ruler of the power of the air and the spirit at work in the sons of disobedience would have named realities the Ephesians knew and feared. Against that backdrop Paul declares that those once enslaved to such powers have been raised and seated with Christ far above them.
context.canonical: This is one of the clearest statements of salvation by grace through faith in the New Testament, standing alongside Romans 3 to 4 and Titus 3:4-7. It gathers up the biblical themes of human deadness in sin, God's initiating mercy, union with Christ, and new creation, and states them with unusual compactness and force.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that salvation is wholly the work of God's grace, received through faith and never earned, so that boasting is excluded and all glory goes to God, and to establish that the saved are a new creation destined for good works.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is description and declaration of what God has done, not a command to be obeyed. It teaches doctrine that shapes life: because salvation is a gift, pride is excluded; because we are created for good works, obedience follows as the fruit and purpose of grace, not its price.

debates.question: What does this is not from yourselves refer to in verse 8?

views.label: The whole of salvation by grace
views.case: The nearest sense is that the entire event, being saved by grace through faith, is God's gift, not something originating in us; the phrase sums up the whole rescue as undeserved and God given.

views.label: Faith itself as the gift
views.case: Some read the gift as faith specifically, so that even the faith by which we receive grace is granted by God, ruling out any ground for boasting in our believing.

debates.bearing: On either reading the point stands: salvation, including our capacity to respond, is God's gift and not a human achievement, so no one can boast; the difference is one of emphasis, not of doctrine.

debates.question: How do good works relate to salvation in verses 9 and 10?

views.label: Works are the result, not the ground
views.case: Verse 9 excludes works as the basis of salvation, while verse 10 names good works as the goal: we are created in Christ for them, and God prepared them beforehand as our way of life.

views.label: Faith and works held together
views.case: Others stress that genuine saving faith necessarily produces the good works God prepared, so that works, though never earning salvation, are its inevitable and intended fruit and the evidence of new creation.

debates.bearing: Both agree that good works flow from salvation rather than achieving it; grace saves, and the saved are remade to walk in the works God has already prepared.

study.originalLanguages: The passage sets deadness against grace. We were nekros, dead, corpses in trespasses and sins, unable to save ourselves, walking under the world, the ruler of the power of the air, and the flesh, children of wrath by nature. Then but God, rich in mercy, made us alive, and Paul cries out that by charis, grace, God's free and unearned favor, you have been saved. He uses the perfect tense of sozo, you have been saved, an accomplished rescue that abides. This salvation is the doron, the gift, of God, not from ourselves and not by works, so no one can boast. And the saved are God's poiema, His workmanship or masterpiece, ktizo, created afresh, in Christ Jesus for the good works He prepared in advance as our way of life.

typology.type: Made alive, raised, and seated with Christ
typology.fulfillment: Unites the believer's story to Christ's death, resurrection, and enthronement, so that His victory in Ephesians 1 becomes the shared possession of His people.
Echoes the first creation of Genesis, when God made humanity good; now God performs a new creation, bringing life out of the death that sin had brought.

typology.type: Children of wrath made children of God
typology.fulfillment: Reverses the exile and condemnation flowing from the fall, as those under wrath are shown the surpassing riches of grace and remade as God's own handiwork.

study.christ: Christ is the one with whom and in whom every saving act occurs. We are made alive with Christ, raised up with Christ, and seated with Him in the heavenly realms, sharing the resurrection and enthronement that chapter one ascribed to Him. He is the ground of God's kindness toward us and the sphere of the new creation, for we are created in Christ Jesus. Apart from union with Him there is only death; in Him there is life, exaltation, and a purpose of good works prepared by God.
study.god: This passage is a portrait of the saving God. He is rich in mercy and moved by His own great love, acting toward us while we were still dead and deserving wrath. Salvation originates entirely in His grace and comes as His gift, so that He alone is glorified and no one can boast. His purpose stretches into the coming ages, that He might forever display the surpassing riches of His grace in His kindness toward us in Christ Jesus. Here is a God who saves the helpless and delights to be known as gracious.

crossReferences.note: While we were still weak and sinners, Christ died for us, showing God's love to the ungodly, matching the but God of verse 4.
God made us alive together with Christ and forgave all our trespasses, a close parallel to verses 4 and 5.
God saved us not by works of righteousness but according to His mercy, echoing salvation by grace, not works.
The resurrection power that raised and seated Christ is the same power now applied to believers made alive and seated with Him.

study.oneStory: These verses tell the whole Bible's story in small: humanity dead and under wrath through the fall, and God's rescuing mercy breaking in with but God. The new creation begun here, people made alive, raised, and seated with Christ, is a foretaste of the day when God makes all things new. The saved are His workmanship, remade to walk in the good works He prepared, a living sign of the redeemed humanity the whole story has been moving toward in Christ.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe our condition before God's saving work?

options.text: Dead in trespasses and sins, enslaved and by nature children of wrath.
options.note: Verses 1 to 3 describe spiritual death, bondage, and wrath.

options.text: Basically healthy but needing a little improvement.
options.note: Paul says we were dead, not merely in need of self-help.

options.text: Weak and struggling, but able to save ourselves with effort.
options.note: The dead cannot revive themselves; only God can make them alive.

options.text: Already reconciled to God apart from Christ.
options.note: We were under wrath and dead in sin until God intervened.

quiz.q: What are the pivotal words that turn the passage in verse 4?

options.text: But God, who is rich in mercy.
options.note: The great reversal hinges on God's merciful intervention.

options.text: But we, who tried our best.
options.note: The turn is God's action, not human effort.

options.text: But the law, which set us free.
options.note: It is God's mercy, not the law, that rescues.

options.text: But time, which healed our wounds.
options.note: Deliverance comes from God's mercy, not the passing of time.

quiz.q: On what basis are we saved, according to verse 8?

options.text: By grace, through faith, as the gift of God.
options.note: Salvation is grace received by faith, God's gift from start to finish.

options.text: By grace plus our own meritorious works.
options.note: Verse 9 explicitly excludes works, so no one can boast.

options.text: By our sincerity and religious devotion.
options.note: It is God's gift, not from ourselves.

options.text: By keeping the law perfectly.
options.note: Salvation is not by works but by grace through faith.

quiz.q: Why does Paul stress that salvation is not by works?

options.text: So that no one can boast; all glory belongs to God.
options.note: Verse 9 excludes boasting because salvation is God's gift.

options.text: So that good works no longer matter at all.
options.note: Works still matter as the goal of salvation, verse 10, just not its ground.

options.text: So that we can feel superior to those who work.
options.note: The point is humility before God, not pride over others.

options.text: Because human effort earns a share of the credit.
options.note: The whole aim is that no one can boast; God gets the glory.

quiz.q: What does it mean that we are God's workmanship?

options.text: We are His handiwork, created anew in Christ for good works.
options.note: Verse 10 calls us God's poiema, created in Christ for the works He prepared.

options.text: We craft ourselves into better people by our own design.
options.note: We are made by God, not self-made.

options.text: We are a finished project with nothing more to live for.
options.note: We are created for good works, a purpose God has prepared.

options.text: We are saved in order to earn our standing by works.
options.note: Good works are the result of salvation, not its price.

quiz.q: How do the good works of verse 10 relate to salvation?

options.text: They are the result and goal of salvation, prepared by God beforehand.
options.note: We are created in Christ for good works God prepared in advance.

options.text: They are the price we pay to be saved.
options.note: Salvation is a gift, not by works; the works follow it.

options.text: They are optional extras with no real place in the Christian life.
options.note: They are the very purpose for which we were created in Christ.

options.text: They earn a higher rank of salvation over time.
options.note: Works flow from grace as its fruit, not as a way to climb higher.

questions: Paul says we were dead in sin, not merely weak. How does seeing salvation as being raised from death, rather than helped along, change the way you view God's grace to you?
The passage turns on two words, but God. Where in your life do you need to hear that but God interrupt a story that felt hopeless?
Salvation is a gift, not a wage, so that no one can boast. Where are you still tempted to think God accepts you because of what you do rather than because of His grace?
You are seated with Christ in the heavenly realms even now. How does sharing in His exaltation change the way you face today's struggles and fears?
You are God's workmanship, created for good works He prepared in advance. How does knowing your good works are planned by God free you to serve without pressure to earn His love?

translationNotes.note: Children of wrath renders a Hebrew style idiom meaning those who are objects of wrath by nature, describing our standing under God's just judgment apart from grace, not that God delights in anger.
You have been saved uses the perfect tense, marking salvation as a completed action with continuing effect; the exclamation breaks into the sentence before the fuller statement in verse 8.
This not from yourselves most likely refers to the whole of salvation by grace through faith rather than to faith alone; either way the point is that salvation is entirely God's gift.

meta.notes: BSB text for Ephesians 2:1-10 reproduced as supplied. No dashes were present to convert. Inline tokens mark the six supplied lexicon keys, each used once.
